one_line_takeaway: CITED protects GNN intellectual property against model-extraction theft by verifying ownership through intrinsic decision-boundary 'signature' nodes that work at both embedding and label levels without injected watermarks or auxiliary models, beating watermarking and fingerprinting baselines on 7 datasets.
key_finding: CITED achieves state-of-the-art ownership verification at both output levels while preserving (and sometimes improving) downstream accuracy. At the embedding level (ARUCemb) it beats the fingerprint baseline GrOVe on all 7 datasets, with the largest gap on PubMed (62.76 vs 37.09) and CS (70.76 vs 57.38). At the label level (ARUClabel) it dominates all three watermark baselines by a wide margin, e.g. CiteSeer 59.29 vs the best baseline ~24.98, and PubMed 58.16 vs at most 19.78. Utility (Table 1) is maintained or improved -- e.g. GCN on CiteSeer 72.03%% vs ~68-69%% for baselines -- whereas watermark methods degrade accuracy as more indicator nodes are injected (Figure 1). The ablation (Table 3) shows the boundary-thickness component is individually the strongest, and combining margin+thickness+heterogeneity gives the best result (ARUCemb 71.07, ARUClabel 54.87). CITED is also substantially faster than GrOVe at inference because it needs no auxiliary models (Figure 2 left).
core_contribution: CITED (DeCision boundary-aware sIgnaTure for model Extraction Defense) is an ownership-verification framework for graph neural networks that introduces a new type of ownership indicator called a 'signature': a subgraph of boundary-sensitive nodes plus their target-model outputs. Unlike watermarking (label-level only, injects task-irrelevant triggers) and fingerprinting (embedding-level only, needs auxiliary reference models), CITED is claimed to be the first method verifying ownership at BOTH the embedding and label levels simultaneously, without harming downstream accuracy and without training extra auxiliary models. The signature arises intrinsically from the model's own decision boundary rather than from injected triggers, so it is implicitly transferred into a surrogate when an attacker queries boundary-adjacent samples during a model extraction attack. The authors also extend the standard ARUC verification metric with a 2-Wasserstein distance for embedding-level matching and provide theoretical bounds (Theorems 4.1 and 4.2) that under bounded parameter perturbation, a surrogate stays close in embedding space and agrees on labels with high probability.
threat_model: Model Extraction Attacks (MEAs) against GNNs deployed as Graph-Machine-Learning-as-a-Service. A target model fT exposes either predicted labels or intermediate node embeddings via API. An adversary repeatedly queries with subgraph inputs (Vquery subset of V, Equery subset of E; transductive setting) and uses the input-output pairs to train a cheap surrogate fS replicating fT's functionality. The defender's goal is ownership verification: given a suspicious model fQ (possibly a stolen surrogate, possibly an independently trained model fI), decide whether fQ was extracted from fT by comparing outputs on the secret signature node set against a matching threshold sigma. The concrete attack used to stress-test the defense is GNNStealing (Shen et al., 2022).
limitations: Scope is restricted to the transductive node-classification setting where the attacker's query graph is a subgraph of the original graph; inductive/other tasks (e.g. link prediction, graph classification) are not evaluated. Only one concrete extraction attack (GNNStealing) is used to instantiate the threat model, so robustness against adaptive attackers who know the signature scheme, or who avoid boundary-region queries, is untested. Results are not uniformly dominant: on some backbone/dataset combinations in the utility table CITED is not the best (e.g. GAT/PubMed, SAGE/Photo), and label-level ARUC values remain modest in absolute terms (37-59) with large variance (e.g. Cora ARUClabel 47.07 +/- 11.3). The theoretical guarantees hold only in the 'small parameter perturbation' regime, which may not capture surrogates trained from scratch on very different architectures. No evaluation of removal/evasion attacks (fine-tuning, pruning, or embedding-distortion defenses by the thief) is reported in the main text.

quantitative_results: ARUCemb (embedding-level verification) CITED vs GrOVe: Cora 62.20 vs 55.87; CiteSeer 66.51 vs 63.09; PubMed 62.76 vs 37.09; Photo 65.62 vs 64.31; Computers 65.80 vs 65.09; CS 70.76 vs 57.38; Physics 61.71 vs 48.04 (higher is better)

ARUClabel (label-level) CITED per dataset: Cora 47.07, CiteSeer 59.29, PubMed 58.16, Photo 45.07, Computers 37.20, CS 41.82, Physics 51.71 -- versus best watermark baseline typically 15-37 (e.g. RandomWM CiteSeer 24.98, BackdoorWM Cora 32.84)

Downstream accuracy (Table 1) example, GCN backbone, CITED: Cora 82.30%%, CiteSeer 72.03%%, PubMed 81.97%%, Photo 93.50%%, Computers 86.63%%, CS 89.23%%, Physics 94.47%% -- competitive with or above watermark/fingerprint baselines
Ablation (Table 3): CITED margin-only ARUCemb 69.67 / ARUClabel 42.00; thickness-only 70.73 / 53.60; heterogeneity-only 66.00 / 16.40; full M+T+H 71.07 / 54.87
PubMed ARUC curve reported at ARUC=0.635 (Figure 2 right); CITED shown substantially lower inference time than GrOVe across datasets (Figure 2 left)
